### DTx name: Managing Depression {#dtx-name:-managing-depression}

#### **Hero Image:**

[01\_Depression\_DTxHeroImage\_Photo.jpg](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1pky17xji6u8VWZg3cPA-g7wh-GPdTJl5/view?usp=drive_link)

#### 

#### **Short description (for tile on homepage, for ex):**  {#short-description-(for-tile-on-homepage,-for-ex):}

CBT tools for depression

**Opt out content:**  
Name: Managing Depression {{dtxProgramName}} program

System Name: DTx Managing Depression

Opt Out Body Text:   
Do you want to stop receiving communications from the Managing Depression {{dtxProgramName}} program?

**Comment Required:** No

#### **Get Started page:** {#get-started-page:}

Intro Video & thumbnail: 

| Video Link | Video Thumbnail |
| :---- | :---- |
| [https://drive.google.com/file/d/1mlbo5ARlvmDXB9RoIju4gLNXfH7jcYZr/view?usp=drive\_link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1mlbo5ARlvmDXB9RoIju4gLNXfH7jcYZr/view?usp=drive_link) | [Depression Intro Video Thumbnail.jpg](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1XWTqT7vEMFgmQY9urC_SlOziH0FLIJHQ/view?usp=drive_link) |

You are not alone. 332 million people around the world have depression and 18.5% of adults in the U S are experiencing symptoms of depression at any given time. There are effective treatments to help you manage depression and get back to your life. 

\<button\>

#### (headline) What you’ll learn:  {#(headline)-what-you’ll-learn:}

Use Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) techniques to identify distorted thoughts, process challenging emotions and develop coping strategies. 

#### 

#### (headline) Who is this for: {#(headline)-who-is-this-for:}

This program is for anyone experiencing symptoms of depression.

\<Talk to your doctor structural content goes here\>

---

---

## Session 1  {#session-1}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:}

Understanding Depression

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:}

Session 1

#### Session description:  {#session-description:}

Learn what contributes to depression and its symptoms.

### Session1 Module1 (S1M1) {#session1-module1-(s1m1)}

#### Module type: {#module-type:}

Education

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:}

What is Depression?

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:}

At any given time, 18.5% of adults in the U S are experiencing symptoms of depression and 5.7% of the global population is experiencing clinical depression. Depression doesn’t discriminate. A person of any age or circumstance can experience depression.

While everyone feels down sometimes, depression is a more prolonged and intense state. It significantly impairs ability to function in everyday life. 

Symptoms can range from moderate to severe. You may feel sad, sluggish, hopeless or empty. You may have less interest in things you enjoy or struggle to do anything at all. Some people experience feelings of anger or irritability. Others report physical symptoms, such as chronic pain or headaches. 

These symptoms must not be a result of a medical condition or substance abuse. Depression may occur with other psychiatric disorders, such as generalized anxiety disorder and insomnia. 

To be diagnosed as depressed, an individual must have symptoms for more than 2 weeks and significant impairment in the functions of daily living. However, you do not need a clinical diagnosis to benefit from this program. To learn more, see the Clinical Diagnosis Criteria for Depression resource. 

If you are experiencing thoughts of harming yourself or suicide, contact emergency services in your local area. In the U S, call 911, call or text 988 to reach the 24-hour National Suicide Helpline or go to an emergency room.

\<button\> Mark as done

Resource:   
[Clinical Diagnosis Criteria for Depression](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1CxhJt6aA-X1sndL0SXp8kC6NtjMugvbMeDLrmf2Wfjo/edit)

---

### S1M2 {#s1m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-1}

Assessment

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-1}

Depression Assessment

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-1}

Answer the questions to determine your baseline symptoms. At the end of the program, you will repeat the assessment to see how your symptoms have changed.

\<button\> Take assessment

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:}

{{Toptext}} Over the last 2 weeks, how often have you been bothered by the following problems?

Little interest or pleasure in doing things

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Feeling down, depressed or hopeless 

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much 

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Feeling tired or having little energy

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Poor appetite or overeating 

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Feeling bad about yourself — or that you’re a failure or have let yourself or your family down

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Trouble concentrating on things, such as reading the newspaper or watching television 

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed. Or the opposite — being so fidgety or restless that you have been moving around a lot more than usual

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself

* Not at all  
* Several days  
* More than half the days  
* Nearly every day

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

### S1M3 {#s1m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-2}

Education

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-2}

A New View of Depression

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-2}

Sayings like “Turn that frown upside down,” or “It’s all good,” express our culture’s over-valuation of optimism and the appearance of happiness. As a result, some people may feel ashamed when struggling with depression. However, depression is not your fault. Many factors, including genetics and unanticipated loss, are entirely outside of your control. 

#### 

#### (headline) There is hope {#(headline)-there-is-hope}

Understanding how depression works is the first step toward feeling better. Depression is one of the best understood mood disorders, and there are many strategies and treatments to manage depression. In this program, you will learn CBT tools and build a toolbox to help you move forward. 

#### 

#### (headline) The latest on depression {#(headline)-the-latest-on-depression}

Psychologists are shifting to view depression as something you “do,” versus “have” or “feel.” This does not mean that depression is your fault. Rather, depression often leads you to do exactly the opposite of what would make you feel better. For example, when you are depressed, you are more likely to isolate from friends and family, resulting in intensified feelings of isolation and loneliness. 

Every concept or insight that you learn from this program will include an opportunity to “put an action on it.” This means asking: “How am I going to turn this strategy or concept into an action I can take to manage my depression symptoms?” You are doing something right now as you engage with this {{session}} and begin to understand how depression works and what you can do about it. 

#### 

#### (headline) Try this strategy {#(headline)-try-this-strategy}

One simple strategy, called “opposite action,” suggests doing the opposite of what your depression is telling you to do. So if you are choosing not to go out with friends because you “will just be a downer” or “don’t have the energy,” use the simple opposite action rule and try doing the opposite. You might be surprised that doing this actually makes you feel better or more energetic, and you may even enjoy yourself. 

#### (headline) Key insights: {#(headline)-key-insights:}

1. Our culture tends to overvalue optimism and positivity, which can lead people to feel ashamed of depression. Depression is NOT your fault.   
2. Depression often leads us to behave in ways that make us more depressed, leading to a vicious cycle.  
3. Try doing the opposite of what depression tells you to do. Changing your behavior is a critical tool for managing your depression. 

\<button\> Mark as done

---

### S1M4  {#s1m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-3}

Exercise 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-3}

Depression or Grief

#### Body:  {#body:-3}

It is important to distinguish depression from feelings of sadness or grief. We all experience sadness and grief in response to life’s challenges, such as the death of a parent or losing your job. Grief is a process that allows us to adjust and come to terms with loss. When someone is grieving, they tend to focus on the past. If a person is unable to move back into normal life activities, grief can lead to an episode of depression. 

If you are experiencing grief or loss, the tools and strategies covered in this program can help you navigate your way through this challenging time. 

Compare the differences between grief and depression and answer the questions to determine if you are experiencing grief or depression. 

####  (headline) **Grief:** {#(headline)-grief:}

* Feelings of sadness about a recent loss   
* Feelings of longing for the past or nostalgia   
* Thoughts or images of being reunited with a lost loved one  
* Difficulty accepting a recent loss  
* Feelings of irritability and anger  
* Difficulty eating and sleeping

These feelings may become less intense with time, but return when reminded of the loss.

#### (headline) **Depression:** {#(headline)-depression:}

* Prolonged feelings of sadness and guilt unrelated to a recent loss  
* Difficulty mobilizing to do normal daily activities  
* Feelings of hopelessness about the future  
* Feelings of worthlessness   
* Morbid thoughts  
* Thoughts of self-harm  
* Feelings of irritability or anger  
* Difficulty eating and sleeping

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-1}

Are you experiencing any feelings of grief? (select all that apply)

* Feelings of sadness about a recent loss   
* Feelings of longing or nostalgia   
* Thoughts or images of being reunited with a lost loved one  
* Difficulty accepting a recent loss  
* None of the above

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

Do these feelings sometimes feel less intense, but return when you are reminded of a loss?

* Yes  
* No  
* N/A

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Based on your answers to the previous questions, what do you think you are experiencing?

* Grief  
* Depression  
* Grief and depression  
* None of the above

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Resources:  
[Types of Depression](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ZsSlx49Q9xt1WvtAOF6j7st7dCQHRoJrQBxI7W-kJQI/edit)  
---

### S1M5  {#s1m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-4}

Exercise 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-4}

What Contributes to Depression?

#### Body:  {#body:-4}

Research has found that major life transitions, such as parenthood, transitioning from student life, career changes and retirement can trigger a depressive episode. Other life stressors that may contribute to depression include moving to a different state or country, losing a job, a breakup or divorce, loss of a loved one, a legal dispute, financial stressors, physical disabilities or health problems.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-2}

Are you currently experiencing any of the following major life transitions? (select all that apply)

* Marital or relationship challenges  
* Becoming a parent  
* Transitioning to or from student life  
* Changing jobs or careers  
* Financial hardship   
* Retirement  
* Chronic illness or physical impairment  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what major life transition are you experiencing? (optional)  
\[Open Answer with validation 26\]

Which of the following is causing stress in your life? (select all that apply)

* Moving  
* Losing a job   
* A break-up or divorce   
* The loss of a loved one   
* A legal dispute  
* Financial difficulties  
* Physical disabilities or health problems  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

How do these changes and stressors affect your mood? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Are you getting the support you need to navigate these transitions? (optional)

* Yes  
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

If no, what could you do to increase your support? What might you be able to do to make them more manageable? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Who is one person in your life that you could talk to about these stressors? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]  
---

### S1M6 {#s1m6}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-5}

Exercise 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-5}

Other Contributing Factors

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-5}

Decades of research on depression have shown us that a depressive episode is the result of many different biological, historical and situational factors.

#### (headline) **Biological Factors** {#(headline)-biological-factors}

* Genetic history of anxiety, depression or mood disorder  
* Brain chemistry   
* Hormonal changes due to pregnancy, childbirth, menopause, etc.  
* Chronic illness or pain  
* Medical problems that cause depressed mood

#### (headline) **Historical Factors** {#(headline)-historical-factors}

* Childhood trauma  
* Family history of complex trauma  
* Trauma, such as witnessing a natural disaster or being victim of a crime  
* History of sexual or domestic abuse

#### (headline) **Situational Factors** {#(headline)-situational-factors}

* Past and current life stressors   
* Relational conflict  
* Lack of social support  
* Stress at work or school  
* Economic stressors such as financial loss or worry  
* Societal stressors, such as political conflicts or health crises

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-3}

What factors may be contributing to your current experience of depression?

* Family history of depression  
* Hormonal changes  
* Chronic illness or pain  
* Childhood trauma  
* Witnessed or victim of a crime or natural disaster  
* Relational conflict  
* Lack of social support  
* Stress at work or school  
* Economic stressors  
* Societal stressors   
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what factors may be contributing to your current experience of depression? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S1M7 {#s1m7}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-6}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-6}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:}

1. Society tends to overvalue optimism and positivity, which can lead us to feel ashamed of our struggle with depression. Depression is NOT your fault.   
2. Depression is one of the most well-researched and common mental disorders, experienced by people from every walk of life.  
3. Depression can be caused by a multitude of factors, like genetics, hormonal changes, past trauma and current stressors and transitions.  
4. Depression often leads us to behave in ways that make us more depressed, leading to a vicious cycle.  
5. Try doing the opposite of what depression tells you to do. Changing your behavior is a critical tool for managing your depression.

#### 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:}

Review the list of what you learned. Select the items you are willing to do to **put an action on it**. You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-4}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Remember that depression is not my fault and not a weakness or character flaw   
* Reflect on the unique factors that may contribute to my depression  
* Talk to my family or friends about what I have learned   
* Try the opposite action tool when I notice that I am isolating myself or avoiding activities  
* Make time to continue this program  
* Talk to my healthcare provider about my depression and rule out medical problems that may be impacting my mood symptoms  
* Consider finding a therapist in my area  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

## Session 2 {#session-2}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-1}

What is CBT?

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-1}

Session 2

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-1}

Learn what Cognitive Behavioral Therapy is and how it works.

### S2M1 {#s2m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-7}

Education

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-7}

Introduction to CBT

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-6}

Thousands of studies have helped us understand how depression works and what strategies are effective to treat it. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is considered the “gold standard” treatment for depression. CBT is different from other “talk therapies” because its goal is to teach you tools and strategies to manage your mood and move through depression.

CBT’s foundational practice is identifying and challenging inaccurate and unhelpful thoughts and considering more helpful alternative thoughts. CBT emphasizes that to change how we feel, we need to be committed to making changes to our behavior. This speaks to the “put an action on it” and “opposite action” concepts you learned in {{session}} 1\. Newer forms of CBT also include self-compassion and mindfulness practices to help you to relate to your emotions in a more flexible and resilient way.

This program will walk you through clinically-proven CBT tools. Each {{lesson}} is designed as an opportunity to learn or practice something that will help you to manage your depression. 

#### (headline) Key insights: {#(headline)-key-insights:-1}

1. Depression has been studied extensively, and CBT is considered one of the most effective treatments.  
2. CBT’s foundational practice is identifying and challenging inaccurate and unhelpful thoughts and considering more helpful alternative thoughts.   
3. CBT also asserts that behavior impacts mood and depression. Therefore, changing your behavior can improve your mood.  
4. Self-compassion and mindfulness practices can also help you to relate to your emotions in a more flexible and resilient way.

\<button\> Mark as done

Resources:  
[How Depression is Treated](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1BAymsLJjvmlSd_2ssoP-9dfkWW9ueeLQqf_LVnrQF7E/edit?usp=sharing)

### ---

### S2M2 {#s2m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-8}

Tracking

**Audio:**  
No

\# Required:  
3

Max per day:  
3

Tracking content:  
Log at least 3 times this week

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-8}

Mood Tracking

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-7}

Tracking your progress is an important aspect of this program. No depression looks the same. Tracking your mood will help you to get to know your unique version of depression. Once you get in the habit of recording your mood, you may notice changes as you begin to try the tools and strategies. Consider noting a few key aspects of your day or how you feel physically in your mood log so you have more context when you look back. 

Each {{session}} outlines concepts, exercises and practices that will help you to learn about and manage your depression. You may find it helpful to use a journal or notebook as a way to take notes, practice using the tools or write reminders and takeaways that you want to try out during the week. It may also be helpful to use a calendar to help your life feel more manageable as you work with this material.

Take action by tracking your mood 3 times this week.

\<button\> Track mood

Question Page:  
How do you feel today? (select all that apply)

* Happy  
* Sad  
* Angry  
* Nervous/anxious  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what are you feeling? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How intense is your feeling or feelings? (optional)  
\[number picker 1-10\]

What were you doing when you felt this way? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Notes and observations: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

#### ---

#### 

### S2M3 {#s2m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-9}

Education

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-9}

Understanding Depression

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-8}

There are many tools in the CBT model that make it effective. The first tool we have explored is understanding depression. There is a wealth of evidence that says understanding what depression is, how it works, how common it is and how to treat it allows people to normalize their experience.

#### 

#### A quick overview: {#a-quick-overview:}

* Depression is not your fault.  
* Depression can be caused by many factors, like genetics, hormonal changes, past trauma and current stressors and transitions.  
* Depression is one of the most well-researched and common mental disorders.  
* Depression can lead you to choose actions that make you more depressed, such as isolating yourself from friends and loved ones.

The next tool, which increases the effectiveness of CBT, is “willingness to change.” This term refers to your willingness to try new things and change old behaviors. Try to tap into your natural curiosity and inquisitiveness when approaching the exercises and tasks in this program. Are you willing to try to think and do your way to feeling better?

#### (header) Key insights: {#(header)-key-insights:}

1. The first CBT tool we learned is understanding depression.  
2. The next CBT tool for your toolbox is a willingness to change.

\<button\> Mark as done  
---

### S2M4 {#s2m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-10}

Exercise

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-10}

The CBT Model

#### Body: {#body:-9}

The 3 areas of change targeted in CBT treatment are thoughts, actions and emotions. A change in one area will impact the others. For instance, changing your thinking is likely to change how you feel and behave, and changing what you do is likely to change how you think and feel. 

![][image1]  
[CBT Diagram\_003.png](https://drive.google.com/file/d/14bps9EoglHheziURMrvoVvjH8iBq08lJ/view?usp=share_link)  
For example, a person starting this program may have the **thought:** “I’m going to feel like this forever.” This **thought** could lead to **feelings** of hopelessness, sadness and discouragement. These feelings could then lead to **behavior** such as isolating oneself and avoiding sources of help. This behavior confirms and reinforces the **thought** “I am going to feel like this forever,” creating a cycle.

However, a more helpful **thought**, such as “This program sounds like it could be helpful; I am going to try it,” may lead to **feelings** of curiosity and hopefulness, which may help motivate you to continue this program. 

The key is to notice how your thinking impacts your emotions and behaviors. Moving forward, you will learn how to balance your thoughts so that they are more helpful and accurate. 

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-5}

Take a moment to think back to a recent situation when you were feeling down. What thoughts do you remember having about this situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What were you feeling?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What did you do?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Can you see how your thoughts impacted your feelings and behavior in this situation?

* Yes  
* No  
* I don’t know

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]  
---

### S2M5  {#s2m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-11}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-11}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### Body: {#body:-10}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-1}

1. Depression has been studied extensively, and CBT is considered one of the most effective treatments.  
2. The first CBT tool we learned is understanding what depression is, how it works, how to treat it and how common it is to normalize your experience.  
3. Willingness to change behaviors and try new things is another key tool in the CBT technique.   
4. The next CBT tool for your toolbox is identifying and challenging thoughts that are inaccurate or unhelpful and considering more helpful alternative thoughts.   
5. CBT also says that behavior impacts mood and depression. Changing your behavior can improve your mood.  
6. Newer forms of CBT have integrated the tools of self-compassion and mindfulness practices that will help you to relate to your emotions in a more flexible and resilient way.

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-1}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it**. You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-6}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Track your mood regularly  
* Reflect on your willingness to change   
* Review what you know about depression  
* Consider how your thoughts affect your emotions and behaviors  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]
