## Session 3 {#session-3}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-2}

Mindfulness and Self-compassion

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-2}

Session 3

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-2}

Learn how mindfulness and self-compassion can help you move through your depression.

### S3M1 {#s3m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-12}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-12}

Why Mindfulness

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-11}

Mindfulness is the foundation of self-compassion and current CBT. **Mindfulness is the practice of shifting into the present moment so you are less likely to get hooked by the negative thoughts, difficult emotions and unhappy memories that are often a part of depression.** A large body of research considers mindfulness practices an extremely powerful tool in current treatments for depression, anxiety, sleep and physical health problems. By learning to be more present in our experience, we can better move through pain rather than wrestling with and resisting our experience. 

To practice grounding yourself in the present moment, try one or both of the following mindfulness exercises.

**5 senses exercise**  
Take a moment and shift your attention to your 5 senses and notice:

* 1 thing that you can see with your eyes  
* 1 thing that you can smell with your nose  
* 1 thing that you can feel with your body, like the chair you are sitting on  
* 1 thing that you can hear  
* 1 thing that you can taste, like the taste of coffee lingering in your mouth

**Acknowledging emotion exercise**  
The negative emotions that we experience are amplified by how much we resist them, resulting in prolonged suffering. Instead of resisting negative emotions, practice accepting your emotions as they are, whether negative or positive.

#### 

Take a moment to practice acknowledging and allowing your emotions:

1. Stop right now and ask yourself: What am I feeling? Bored? Tired? Hopeful? Stressed?   
2. Now say: “This is what I am experiencing right now.”   
3. Can you simply sit with the emotions you are having and allow them to be and change as you make space for your emotions moment by moment? 

You can practice this at any time: while you’re in traffic on the way home, at a tedious meeting at work or while rocking your child to sleep. Simply ask yourself to “Be here now” with kindness and without judgment. 

\<button\> Take action

#### Question page: {#question-page:-7}

Which grounding exercise did you practice? 

* 5 senses technique  
* Acknowledging emotions exercise  
* Both

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Notes and observations:   
\[open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S3M2 {#s3m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-13}

Guided practice

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-13}

Mindfulness 101

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-12}

Mindfulness in its simplest form is asking you to show up for yourself in the present moment with kindness and without judgment. Practicing mindfulness allows you to be more receptive to the CBT tools and strategies you are learning and to be gentler with yourself as you work to manage your depression.

#### Mindfulness Tool \#1 – The Breath {#mindfulness-tool-#1-–-the-breath}

The first mindfulness tool couldn’t be simpler: **Breath.** Breathing is a core skill for bringing yourself into the present moment.

#### 

#### (headline) Try the 4-7-8 breathing exercise {#(headline)-try-the-4-7-8-breathing-exercise}

#### 

[4-7-8 breathing exercise video script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Jf_8RdFlX9il-vlE2jgToaESro-mmZM6DT668EJhoWM/edit#heading=h.fuuxlwtzf9oa)

\<button\> Reflect

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-8}

Did this breathing exercise make you feel more grounded in the present moment?

* Yes  
* No   
* I don’t know

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Would you try it again?

* Yes   
* No  
* I don’t know

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Notes and observations: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Resource:

[Basic meditation exercise](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FbElw4njbxw-kzY7R68wLiihKM4zsIeC1zIcnpTjRtQ/edit)  
---

### S3M3  {#s3m3}

#### 

#### Module type: {#module-type:-14}

Guided practice

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-14}

Why Self-Compassion?

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-13}

Self-compassion is simply being kind to yourself — showing up for yourself in the same way you would show up for a friend. Kristin Neff, the researcher who pioneered the study of self-compassion, describes this process as shifting from the “scare system” to the “care system.” This shift is essential for learning how to flexibly roll with difficult emotional experiences. 

#### 

#### (headline) What is the scare system? {#(headline)-what-is-the-scare-system?}

The scare system is the part of our brain that evolved to keep us safe from the terrifying physical dangers our ancestors faced, like dangerous predators or natural disasters. Yet, our brains still treat everyday stressors as threats. This scare system is at the root of the reactive thoughts, feelings and behaviors of depression. Mindfulness and self-compassion allow you to shift out of your scare system and into the care system, where you encourage and support your efforts to manage your depression. Remember, depression isn’t a character flaw or a weakness, it is a common human experience that you can understand and manage. Let’s begin that process with kindness and curiosity.

#### (Headline) Try the affectionate breathing meditation {#(headline)-try-the-affectionate-breathing-meditation}

[Affectionate breathing meditation video script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1WK9TVaM51js4bBBTFid5ZafI-u3RDhRf4c214fapnS8/edit)

\<button\> Reflect

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-9}

Did this meditation make you feel more open to self-compassion?

* Yes  
* No   
* I don’t know

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Would you try it again?

* Yes   
* No  
* I don’t know

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

Notes and observations: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S3M4 {#s3m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-15}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-15}

Be a Friend to Yourself

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-14}

#### 

#### (headline) Noticing the “bully voice” in our head {#(headline)-noticing-the-“bully-voice”-in-our-head}

When you are in the scare system, your reactive brain often begins to bully you, saying harsh and critical things that make you feel worse. When you stop to notice what you say to yourself when you are struggling, you may be surprised to notice phrases you would never say to someone else. If you would like to explore this more, check out the resource.

#### 

#### (headline) Speaking to yourself more kindly  {#(headline)-speaking-to-yourself-more-kindly}

You can do this in a few different ways:

* Self-compassion researcher, Kristin Neff, suggests using a term of endearment with yourself (she uses “darling”).   
* Try using the soothing properties of touch, by placing your hands on your heart or holding your own hand to comfort yourself in challenging times.  
* Consider writing yourself words of encouragement and leaving them in places you will see, like on the bathroom mirror or refrigerator.  
* Do a guided meditation to cultivate and practice self-compassion.   
* Take the time to appreciate yourself with the following exercise.

#### 

#### (headline) Take the time to appreciate yourself {#(headline)-take-the-time-to-appreciate-yourself}

Spend a few minutes writing a list of things that you like about yourself. They don’t have to be huge accomplishments or feats. If you are having a hard time coming up with things to write on your list, ask a friend or family member for some ideas. Are you a loyal friend, a curious cook or an endearingly bad cook? Try it and see what you come up with. Add things to the list as you think of more, and review the list when you are feeling down on yourself.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-10}

Things I like about myself:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Words of encouragement:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Resource:  
[Self-Compassion 20/20](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1_PCkCeVn8BrrVgIP1o6IwS_v-X0v7sg7zv31Aecn4DA/edit)  
---

### S3M5 {#s3m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-16}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-16}

Identify Your Values

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-15}

By facing your depression and trying to do something about it, you have chosen to do something very difficult on purpose because it matters. You know something truly matters to you when you choose to do it even if it is going to be uncomfortable, difficult or boring. We use the term values, but you can simply think of the areas of your life that really matter to you. 

By becoming more aware of your values, you can use them to orient yourself when things are challenging or confusing. Like a beacon at sea when the waters are stormy, our values can keep us moving toward what matters. Sometimes we get off course, but our values will help us to orient back to the important things in our lives. What are the values that will help to guide you when you are a little discouraged or off course?

Values fall into several broader domains. Consider these example values to see which ones resonate with you.

* Relationships/Family   
  * *I value supporting my family financially*  
  * *I value spending time with my family*   
  * *I value showing up for my friends and loved ones*  
* Work/Achievement  
  * *I value hard work and diligence*  
  * *I value excelling at what I do*  
  * *I value professional and academic achievement*  
* Community/Service  
  * *I value showing up for and giving back to my community*  
  * *I value supporting the less fortunate in my community*  
  * *I value caring for the earth and humanity*  
* Play/Leisure  
  * *I value pushing my limits and challenging myself*  
  * *I value travel and learning about other cultures*  
  * *I value taking time to enjoy life and explore my hobbies*  
* Health/Wellness  
  * *I value caring for my body and staying fit*  
  * *I value personal development and growth*  
* Art/Creativity  
  * *I value experiencing art, theater, etc.*  
  * *I value creating new things*   
* Spirituality/Religion  
  * *I value feeling connected to something larger than myself*  
  * *I value my religion or faith*

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-11}

Select the domains that are most important in your life: (recommended to select top 2-3)

* Relationships/Family  
* Work/Achievement  
* Community/Service  
* Play/Leisure  
* Health/Wellness  
* Art/Creativity  
* Spirituality/Religion  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what other values are important to you? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Write a few examples of your values in the areas that you selected. You can use the examples to guide your responses.  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S3M6 {#s3m6}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-17}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-17}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-16}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-2}

1. Mindfulness and self-compassion are foundations of current CBT.  
2. The key to mindfulness is staying grounded in the present moment.  
3. Breathing is a core skill for bringing yourself into the present moment.  
4. The negative emotions that we experience are amplified by how much we resist them, resulting in prolonged suffering. Instead, practice acknowledging and accepting your emotions.  
5. The scare system can cause reactive thoughts, feelings and behaviors of depression. Mindfulness and self-compassion allow you to shift out of your scare system and into the care system, where you encourage and support your efforts to manage your depression.  
6. Knowing what you value can help you stay oriented toward what matters. 

#### 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-2}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it.** You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-12}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice the 5 senses technique to ground yourself  
* Practice acknowledging your emotions   
* Practice the 4-7-8 breathing technique  
* Make a plan to practice mindfulness or meditation regularly  
* Practice being aware when you are overly critical of yourself   
* Write words of encouragement to yourself  
* Revisit your list of accomplishments when you are feeling down  
* Revisit your list of values to stay on track  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

## Session 4 {#session-4}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-3}

Thoughts

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-3}

Session 4

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-3}

Learn to identify your negative automatic thoughts. 

### S4M1 {#s4m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-18}

Education

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-18}

Introduction to Your Thoughts

#### Body: {#body:-17}

Negative thinking is the hallmark symptom of depression and the most impactful place to begin. Dr. Aaron Beck developed CBT based on his observations of the persistent negative thinking in his depression patients. By helping his patients to recognize their negative thoughts and consider shifting to more helpful and accurate thoughts, he was able to improve their depression symptoms. This skill is called “thought restructuring,” and there are many ways to practice this strategy.  

The difficult emotions that are a part of depression, such as sadness, hopelessness and irritability, can feel like they appear without warning. Extensive research has demonstrated that our feelings are usually linked to thoughts we are having about ourselves, others and the world around us. By becoming more aware of the “negative automatic thoughts,” or “NATs,” it is easier to make sense of the challenging emotions that accompany them. 

Thoughts are powerful mediators of our experience, yet when we stop and examine them, they are frequently inaccurate and unhelpful. The first step toward changing how you feel is identifying the thoughts that underlie your mood. The next step is to begin to evaluate these thoughts for accuracy and helpfulness. And then we can begin to consider more balanced and helpful thoughts that may change how we are feeling. By challenging your thoughts and considering more accurate and helpful thoughts, you can change how you feel. 

#### (headline) Key insights: {#(headline)-key-insights:-2}

1. Difficult feelings are usually linked to negative thoughts about ourselves, others and the world around us.  
2. Noticing negative automatic thoughts (NATs) can help us to recognize that our thinking is often inaccurate and unhelpful. We can consider more helpful and accurate ways to look at situations that may help us to feel differently.

\<button\> Mark as done

#### ---

### S4M2 {#s4m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-19}

Guided practice

**Audio:**  
No

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-19}

Catching Negative Thinking

#### Body: {#body:-18}

Our thoughts are often overly negative and inaccurate. Negative automatic thoughts (we will call them NATs) are the hallmark symptom of depression. This is why a core CBT strategy is learning to notice and identify negative thoughts. This skill is the first step toward understanding how your thoughts are directly linked to how you feel.

#### 

#### (headline) Try this exercise  {#(headline)-try-this-exercise}

[Catch negative thoughts video script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/11VxTnd6Uw7WAHLTV5QeaKEFVkB3UH-FNE3c0N6N2F6w/edit)

\<button\> Reflect

#### Question page: {#question-page:-13}

Here are some negative thoughts that are common to depression. Select all that are familiar to you:

* I always screw things up  
* It’s all on me  
* I have to be perfect or I won’t be loved  
* Bad things always happen to me  
* It’s hopeless  
* No one understands me  
* Things always go wrong for me  
* I will never find someone who loves me  
* I will never feel good again  
* I always get the short end of the stick  
* Things are harder for me than other people   
* I will never be good enough  
* None of the above

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

What feelings are linked to the thoughts you selected? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Are there other NATs that you experience that contribute to your experience of depression? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S4M3 {#s4m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-20}

Education

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-20}

Common Thinking Traps

#### Body: {#body:-19}

Research shows that there are certain types of “thinking traps” that all of us sometimes engage in. When you are depressed, you are particularly inclined to fall into these thinking traps. Knowing how to identify these thinking traps will help you recognize when your thinking may be overly negative.

* **Catastrophizing** – Jumping straight to the worst-case scenario when evaluating a situation.  
  **Example:** When you make a mistake at work, you believe that your boss is going to fire you. When you have a minor setback in your life, you conclude that “My life is a total disaster.”

* **All-or-nothing thinking** – Seeing things in extreme terms and ignoring the wide spectrum of possibilities between the extremes. This thinking trap is easy to recognize by the presence of the terms “always” or “never.”  
  **Example:**  Things are either amazing or terrible, exhilarating or a drag. “If I don’t always get perfect grades, I am a failure.” 

* **Personalization** – Feeling responsible for things that are outside of your control. Assuming that normal life situations are the result of others thinking negatively of you.  
  **Example**: Assuming that your friend doesn’t care about you because they don’t respond to your text. Feeling like you are personally responsible for the poor performance of the company you work for.

* **Discounting the positive** – Overvaluing negative information that supports your negative thoughts and beliefs, while devaluing positive and neutral information.  
  **Example**: Fixating on the one awkward moment during an otherwise enjoyable date.   
    
* **Emotional reasoning** – Mistaking feelings for facts. Using your feelings to draw rational conclusions.  
  **Example:** “I feel like a failure, therefore I am a failure.” “I feel like she doesn’t care about me, therefore she doesn’t care about me.” When you are feeling hopeless, you assume that the situation *is* hopeless.  
    
* **Mind reading** – Believing that we know what others are thinking and basing our actions on this assumption.  
  **Example:** “I know she doesn’t like me, so I ignored her at the party.”

\<button\> Mark as done

---

### S4M4 {#s4m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-21}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-21}

Start a Thought Record

#### Body: {#body:-20}

Learning to notice and record your NATs can help you become more aware of how your thinking impacts your mood. CBT therapists sometimes use “thought records” to practice this skill. As you become more aware of your negative thoughts, you can begin considering alternative ways of thinking about situations. Noticing your thoughts and connecting them to a feeling is the first step. When you become more aware of your negative thinking, the negative emotions of depression are easier to understand.

Think back to a situation in the past few days when you were experiencing difficult emotions. Reflect on the situation and identify the thoughts and feelings that accompanied it. See the resource for an example.

**~~Situation \-~~** ~~Describe the situation, answering the questions:~~

* ~~What happened?~~  
* ~~When and where did it happen?~~  
* ~~Who was there?~~  
* ~~Why did it happen?~~

*~~Situation example: “I lost my job this week.”~~*

**~~NAT \-~~** ~~Identify your negative automatic thought~~  
*~~NAT example: “I am a failure.”~~*

**~~Thinking Error \-~~** ~~Identify the thinking error category your NAT falls under~~  
*~~Thinking Error Example: All-or-Nothing~~*

**~~Emotion \-~~** ~~Identify your mood and rate from 1-10 (1 \= mild, 10 \= intense)~~  
*~~Emotion example: Ashamed \- intensity 5, Hopeless \- intensity 6~~*

Resource:  
[Thought Record Example](https://docs.google.com/document/d/11O7GBZ3a5BTf7bJ4G8onuAUYMkyfrS5yzepWE6LNXto/edit)

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-14}

Explain the situation, answering who, what, when, where and why.  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What NATs did you experience in this situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What kind of thinking trap is your NAT?

* Catastrophizing   
* All-or-nothing thinking  
* Personalization  
* Discounting the positive  
* Emotional reasoning  
* Mind reading 

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

What was your emotion in this situation?   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How intense was your mood on a scale of 1-10, 1 being the least intense and 10 being the most intense?   
\[number picker, 1-10\]

---

### S4M5  {#s4m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-22}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-22}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### Body: {#body:-21}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-3}

1. Difficult feelings are usually linked to negative thoughts about ourselves, others and the world around us.  
2. Noticing NATs can help us to recognize that our thinking is often inaccurate and unhelpful.   
3. Knowing how to identify thinking traps such as catastrophizing, all-or-nothing thinking or personalization will help you recognize when your thinking may be overly negative.  
4. We can consider more helpful and accurate ways to look at situations that may help us to feel differently about the situation.

#### 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-3}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it**. You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-15}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice catching NATs  
* Practice identifying thought errors  
* Practice mindfulness to become more aware of your thoughts  
* Practice creating a thought record to cope with difficult emotions and situations  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, Multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

---

## Session 5:  {#session-5:}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-4}

Helpful Thoughts

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-4}

Session 5

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-4}

Learn to challenge your negative thoughts and consider more accurate and helpful thoughts.

### S5M1 {#s5m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-23}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-23}

Collecting Evidence

#### Body: {#body:-22}

Thoughts are not facts, yet we often overvalue them when evaluating situations. When you believe everything that your brain thinks, you are at the mercy of your very busy and changeable brain. For instance, have you ever thought “This is impossible” or “I will never be able to do this” when facing a challenging task? Was this thought always true, or were you able to face the challenge or do the task? That is why it is important to take a step back and evaluate our thoughts for helpfulness and accuracy. Like a good scientist, collect evidence to support or disprove the accuracy of your thinking.

Research has found that people with depression tend to search for and favor information that supports negative thoughts and beliefs. This reinforces negative thoughts and dismisses evidence to disprove the belief. You can counteract this negative confirmation bias by collecting evidence to disprove the negative thoughts and beliefs.  Use the example in the resource section for reference.

~~Use the example for reference:~~

**~~Situation~~** ~~\- *I lost my job this week.*~~   
**~~NAT \-~~** *~~I am a failure.*~~  
**~~Thinking Error \-~~** *~~All-or-nothing thinking*~~  
**~~Emotion \-~~** *~~Ashamed \- intensity 5, Hopeless \- intensity 6*~~  
**~~Evidence for NAT~~** 

* *~~I failed a class once.~~*

**~~Evidence against NAT~~**

* *~~I completed my degree.~~*  
* *~~I held this job for 5 years.~~*  
* *~~My manager said it wasn’t because of me; it was because of downsizing.~~*  
* *~~I have many transferable skills.~~*

#### 

#### (headline) Key insights: {#(headline)-key-insights:-3}

1. Thoughts are not facts.   
2. Depression creates a negative confirmation bias by favoring evidence that confirms negative thoughts and dismissing evidence that disproves these beliefs.  
3. You can break this cycle by collecting evidence to disprove your NATs and support your more balanced thinking.

Review and use your answers from the thought record exercise in {{session}} 4 to complete this exercise.

Resource:  
[Thought Record with Evidence](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1z0EdnjIyawliNCgaO7RUBQ5EZ0y3GRVi99nhrq4kHIY/edit)

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-16}

Look at your NAT and write the evidence that supports it:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Now write the evidence that disproves the thought:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Usually, we will find that our overly negative thoughts are not supported by the evidence. What did you find? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S5M2  {#s5m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-24}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-24}

Balanced Thinking

#### Body: {#body:-23}

The next step in the cognitive restructuring process is to consider alternative thoughts that are more balanced and accurate. Review the NATs you identified in your thought record, evaluate the evidence to support and disprove them and consider alternative thoughts that may be more helpful. Use the resource as an example.

~~Look at the complete example for reference:~~

**~~Situation:~~** ~~*I lost my job this week.*~~   
**~~NAT:~~** *~~I am a failure.*~~  
**~~Thinking Error:~~** *~~All-or-nothing thinking*~~  
**~~Emotion:~~** *~~Ashamed \- intensity 5, Hopeless \- intensity 6*~~  
**~~Evidence for NAT~~** 

* *~~I failed a class once~~*

**~~Evidence against NAT~~**

*  *~~I completed my degree*~~  
* *~~I held this job for 5 years~~*  
* *~~My manager said it wasn’t because of me; it was because of downsizing~~*  
* *~~I have many transferable skills~~*

**~~Alternative thought:~~** ~~Finding and adjusting to a new job may take time and effort, but I have the skills and support to be successful.~~

In previous {{lessons}}, you practiced identifying an NAT and collecting evidence for and against it. Review your previous answers to help you consider a more balanced, helpful thought.

Resource:  
[Thought Record with Alternatives](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1MGPMicVWVUhTIbj9O8tyY5ou5Aab0cTnra3vBuno1n0/edit)

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-17}

Consider what the best, worst and most likely scenarios are for your situation. Write them out if you like. (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

If the worst-case scenario were to happen, what resources and support would you need? Do you have access to some of these resources? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Considering the evidence and the possible outcomes, what is a more balanced and helpful thought that you could consider in this situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What did you notice when you considered alternative thoughts? Did the process help you to feel differently about the situation? If so, how? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What will remind you to use this strategy in your daily life to help you balance your thinking when you notice you are believing your NATs? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S5M3 {#s5m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-25}

Education

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-25}

Worrying vs. Problem-Solving

#### Body: {#body:-24}

Because negative thinking and thinking traps, like “catastrophizing” and “all-or-nothing thinking,” play a role in depression, it is not surprising that worry is often a part of depression. People often find themselves ruminating about things that happened in the past or might happen in the future. Rumination and worry use the same parts of the brain as problem-solving. As a result, we often mistake worry for problem-solving.

**Worry** is a repetitive pattern of negative thinking about threatening issues that focuses on worst-case scenarios and **does not generate a plan of action**.  
You are probably worrying when:

* You focus more on what *you don’t want* to happen than what *you do want* to happen  
* You focus on things that went wrong in the past or what will go wrong in the future  
* You are using a thinking trap as evidence, such as “I always do horribly when I give presentations”  
* You notice physical scare system symptoms (sweating, racing heart or breath, etc.)


**Problem-solving** is defining a problem, considering possible solutions, weighing pros and cons and **choosing the next course of action.**

Many people mistake worry for constructive thinking. When told that they can reduce their  
worry cycle, people often respond, “But it's my job to worry\! How else will I solve the problem?” Now that we know that isn’t true, we can begin to shift our brains from worry to problem-solving. When you recognize that you are worrying, you can use the skills we have practiced to switch from the scare system to the care system so you can move to a state where problem-solving is possible. 

Then you can begin to problem solve by:

* Defining the problem/goal  
* Generating ideas and solutions  
* List your best ideas and solutions to evaluate the pros and cons   
* Considering your choices and choosing your next best action

\<button\> Mark as done

---

### S5M4 {#s5m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-26}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-26}

Cognitive Defusion 

#### Body: {#body:-25}

Your brain is built to be a “negative thought generating machine.” These thoughts are so familiar to you that you may not even notice them or question their accuracy. Sometimes it is useful to use thought-restructuring techniques like the thought record. But sometimes, a more workable solution is to simply let the thoughts pass through without getting hooked by them. This technique is called cognitive defusion. It involves finding ways to unhook from the unhelpful negative chatter your brain generates. 

To practice this, notice your thoughts and add the words “I am having the thought that” before the thought. So if you are thinking, “I will never finish this project,” this thought would become: “I am having the thought that I will never finish this project.” Or if you have the thought “I am never going to feel better,” it would become: “I am having the thought that I am never going to feel better.” This seems like a simple trick, but give it a try and see what you notice.	

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-18}

Think back to a situation that happened recently and write down some of the thoughts you were having.   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Now add “I was having the thought that…” to the thought. Does that change how you think about the situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Notice your thinking right now. Are any NATs coming up? If so, what are they? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Try the cognitive defusion trick again with these thoughts: “I am having the thought that:\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.” (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What do you notice about using this technique? Do you think you might remember to try this when in a negative thinking loop?   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S5M5 {#s5m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-27}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-27}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### Body: {#body:-26}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-4}

1. Thoughts are not facts.  
2. Depression creates a negative confirmation bias by favoring evidence that confirms negative thoughts and dismissing evidence that disproves these beliefs.  
3. You can break this cycle by collecting evidence to disprove your NATs and support new, more balanced thoughts.  
4. Worry, often mistaken for problem-solving, is a repetitive pattern of negative thinking about threatening issues that focuses on worst-case scenarios and does not generate a plan of action.  
5. Problem-solving is defining a problem, considering possible solutions and choosing the next course of action.  
6. Cognitive defusion is a form of thought restructuring where you let thoughts pass without getting hooked by them. Practice this by saying to yourself “I am having the thought that,” then state the thought. 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-4}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it**. You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-19}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice questioning the accuracy of your thoughts  
* Practice gathering evidence against NATs  
* Practice creating thought records in difficult situations  
* Practice identifying and acknowledging worry  
* Practice shifting from worrying to problem-solving  
* Practice cognitive defusion   
* Other

\[Multiple choice, Multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]  
---

---

## Session 6 {#session-6}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-5}

Emotions

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-5}

Session 6

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-5}

Learn how identifying your emotions can help you accept and manage them.

### S6M1 {#s6m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-28}

Education

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-28}

Introduction to Emotions

#### Body: {#body:-27}

The number 1 reason people seek psychological help is the desire to not feel the way that they are feeling. There isn’t a book, therapist or resource that can rid you of challenging emotions. Difficult emotions are unavoidable, especially if you are living a rich and meaningful life. However, CBT tools such as thought restructuring, increasing your emotional vocabulary, labeling emotions and practicing mindfulness can help you to relate to your emotions in a more flexible way. 

#### 

#### (headline) What are emotions? {#(headline)-what-are-emotions?}

Emotions color our lives and impact our experiences in important ways. They can mobilize us: “Yikes, I’m late, I need to get to work,” or inform us: “I have lost someone I love.” Emotions are a part of every moment of our life like the weather. Sometimes we are more or less aware of them. And naturally, we wish to experience more sunny days than cloudy days. Nonetheless, life inevitably includes difficult emotions that we would rather not have. 

#### 

#### (headline) Dealing with negative emotions {#(headline)-dealing-with-negative-emotions}

Negative emotions are an unavoidable part of life. Learning to recognize your emotions and to link thoughts to emotions is a critical step toward managing your depression. When we are feeling low, it can help to check in and determine which thoughts are impacting your mood. For instance, you may wake up feeling stressed and down. When you check in with your thinking, you may notice that you are having thoughts like: “I hardly slept; today is going to be hard,” or “My boss is visiting today, and she is going to be upset that I haven’t finished the report yet.”

Can you imagine catching yourself as you make a negative prediction about your day and replacing it with an alternative? That could look like: “Today may be a little challenging, but I will make sure to get to bed early tonight so I can make up for last night,” or “My report isn’t done, but the first sections are great, and I will show her those and give her a date when I will finish the rest.” 

#### (headline) Key insights: {#(headline)-key-insights:-4}

1. Many people try to avoid negative emotions, but they are an unavoidable part of the human experience.  
2. A critical step toward managing your depression is learning to recognize your emotions and to link thoughts to emotions.   
3. As you begin to catch negative thoughts, you can practice considering more helpful alternatives.

\<button\> Mark as done  
---

### S6M2 {#s6m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-29}

Guided practice

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-29}

Difficult Emotions

#### Body: {#body:-28}

We often react to difficult emotions, like sadness, anger or irritability, by trying to avoid them. This is our fear system responding to discomfort in the same way it responds to danger. Difficult emotions are challenging, but they are not dangerous. Attempts to avoid the discomfort of difficult emotions can prolong and intensify these difficult emotions. 

Learning to tolerate the discomfort of difficult emotions is a critical part of learning how to relate to our emotions differently. Instead of using all of your energy to avoid challenging emotions, recognizing that they are normal and unavoidable can help you approach your emotions differently. What would it be like to stop, take a breath and get curious about what you are feeling? 

(headline) Try this guided meditation to cultivate some curiosity about your emotions. Then, answer some questions about your meditation experience.

[Guided meditation video script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Toalx1z-vHLZxDoWe2QwXvHJmcJMxfZxuk7LhasuYTI/edit)

#### 

\<button\> Reflect

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-20}

Did this meditation help you approach your emotions with more curiosity?

* Yes   
* No  
* I don’t know

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

What feelings did you notice, and what did you learn by noticing them? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What might change if you were able to relate to your emotions differently? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S6M3 {#s6m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-30}

Education

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-30}

Emotional Vocabulary

#### Body: {#body:-29}

Our emotions affect how we feel and think about our experiences. By learning new ways to describe your emotions, you can begin to view your emotions, and by extension your thoughts and experiences, as more nuanced and complex. In fact, research shows that expanding your emotional vocabulary is a proven strategy for learning to regulate emotions more flexibly. 

Instead of viewing an experience as either good or bad, or something that made you happy or sad, you may notice that your feelings are more complex than that. They can be mixed. For example, you may feel happy at your child’s birthday party. Upon reflection, you may notice that you feel content and fulfilled that your child enjoyed the party, but you may also feel lonely and resentful that you had to plan it all by yourself. 

Feeling a mixture of emotions is normal. By describing our emotions in more descriptive language, we can begin to acknowledge and accept the complexity of our feelings and experiences. 

\<button\> Mark as done

---

### S6M4 {#s6m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-31}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-31}

Get Descriptive

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-30}

Review the core emotions and their more descriptive synonyms. Can you imagine using the more descriptive emotions to describe how you feel?

(headline) Happy  
Content, delighted, fulfilled, energized, engaged, hopeful, optimistic, excited, confident, joyful, amused, peaceful

(headline) Sad  
Lonely, empty, discouraged, disappointed, guilty, unhappy, grief, hopeless, powerless, worthless, weary, helpless, heartbroken, hurt 

(headline) Angry  
Frustrated, irritated, disgusted, furious, resentful, envious, impatient, jealous, shocked, enraged, envious, contempt

(headline) Caring/Connected  
Secure, passionate, caring, safe, empathetic, adoration, compassionate, attracted, comforted, assured, romantic, accepted

(headline) Afraid  
Insecure, anxious, vulnerable, concerned, defensive, frightened, suspicious, shaken, apprehensive, distressed, worried, tense

(headline) Curious  
Open, intrigued, fascinated, engaged, interested, exploring, stimulated, present

(headline) Confused  
Lost, uncertain, embarrassed, trapped, startled, overwhelmed, pessimistic, indecisive, unsure, stuck, frozen

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-21}

Think of a situation that is currently happening or has happened recently and answer the questions. What is the situation?   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What NATs did you have about the situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What emotion were you feeling in this situation?

* Happy   
* Sad  
* Angry  
* Caring/Connected  
* Afraid  
* Curious  
* Confused  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, choose 1\]

If other, what emotion were you feeling? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Look at the emotion that you chose and use the examples to identify a more complex emotion.  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What did you notice when you described the experience in a more complex and descriptive way? Did anything change about how you felt?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Are there any takeaways from this exercise that you could use in your daily life? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What might help you to remember to use this skill? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S6M5  {#s6m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-32}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-32}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### Body: {#body:-31}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-5}

1. Difficult emotions are an unavoidable part of the human experience.  
2. Attempts to avoid the discomfort of difficult emotions can prolong and intensify these difficult emotions.  
3. Learning to be with and tolerate the discomfort of difficult emotions is a critical part of learning how to relate to our emotions differently.   
4. When you expand your emotional vocabulary, you are able to recognize that your emotions are nuanced and complex.   
5. Building our vocabulary for emotions is a proven strategy for learning to regulate emotions in a more flexible way.

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-5}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it.** You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-22}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice linking thoughts to emotions   
* Practice noticing and identifying emotions  
* Practice expanding your emotional vocabulary   
* Practice accepting your emotional experience   
* Practice meditation  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, Multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]  
---

## Session 7 {#session-7}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-6}

Complex Emotions

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-6}

Session 7

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-6}

Learn how to accept and manage complex emotions.

### S7M1 {#s7m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-33}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-33}

Unpacking Complex Emotions

#### Body: {#body:-32}

(headline) Past and future  
Some emotional experiences are hard to move through because they are connected to or based in past experiences or fears we have about the future. For example, you may feel very upset, sad and angry about a simple miscommunication with a friend. When you reflect on it, you may realize this is because a past miscommunication led to a rupture in a friendship and fear this could happen again.

(headline) Judgments and beliefs  
Other times, certain emotions can be difficult to understand because we have beliefs or judgments that they are “bad” or “wrong.” For example, you may have a belief that it is wrong or bad to have a negative emotion. This might make you feel worse about feeling this negative emotion, when in fact, negative emotions are normal and experienced by everyone.

It can be helpful to take a moment to step back and answer some questions that tease apart complicated emotional experiences so we can more flexibly move through them and get back to our lives.

#### 

#### Answer the questions to practice identifying and unpacking complex emotions.  {#answer-the-questions-to-practice-identifying-and-unpacking-complex-emotions.}

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-23}

Describe a situation, either current or in the past, that you may have multiple or complex emotions about:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What emotions were you feeling about this situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Are any of the emotions that you are having toward this event related to something that happened in the past? 

* Yes   
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

If yes, how are these emotions related to the past? What is this memory or event? Is the past situation related or similar to the current situation? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Are the emotions you are having about this situation related to something you fear may happen in the future?  

* Yes  
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose one\]

If yes, how are these emotions related to something you fear may happen in the future? Is the current situation directly related to the feared outcome? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What was it like to unpack your complicated emotional experiences in this way? Did it change how you feel or think about the situation? If so, how? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S7M2 {#s7m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-34}

Exercise

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-34}

Reacting vs. Responding

#### Body: {#body:-33}

#### 

Our emotions are often linked to an automatic reaction. When we are hurt, we withdraw. When we are angry, we may lash out. When we encounter a dangerous predator, we flee. This works well to save you from dangerous situations. 

However, it is problematic when your brain perceives the normal life discomforts as “dangerous” and reacts automatically. Reacting to normal human difficulties with our primitive fear system can make difficult situations even worse and propel us into a cycle of reacting without considering who we want to be in the situation.   
   
Many emotion regulation strategies emphasize the importance of recognizing when the fear system is activated. This technique helps you to reconsider the situation before you respond.  

#### 

#### (headline) Tune in to the physical symptoms of the fear system  {#(headline)-tune-in-to-the-physical-symptoms-of-the-fear-system}

To notice if something activated your fear system, look for the physical symptoms of stress or anxiety in your body. These are different for everyone, so it is important to get acquainted with yours. These symptoms can include sweaty palms, a flushed face, a racing heart and rapid or restricted breathing. Your specific set of symptoms are your “cheat sheet,” or clues that you are moving into the fear system. This means you may need to use some of the emotion regulation skills you are learning in this {{session}} so that you can shift from reacting to responding. 

\<button\> Complete exercise  
 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-24}

Take a look at the list and select your fear system’s physical symptoms.

* Racing heart  
* Flushed face  
* Dilated pupils  
* Shaking or trembling  
* Sweaty palms  
* Rapid or restricted breathing  
* Dry mouth  
* Tingling in arms and legs

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

When you experience these symptoms, what steps could you take to prevent an automatic reaction? (optional)  
\[open answer with validation 26\]

#### ---

### S7M3  {#s7m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-35}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-35}

The STOP Technique

#### Body: {#body:-34}

The next time you are noticing that you are in “fear mode,” try the STOP technique to guide you away from reacting and toward a more measured response.

1. **S** – **Stop** when you recognize you’re in a “hot” state.  
2. **T** – **Take** a breath.  
3. **O** – **Observe** the situation from a distance.  
4. **P** – **Proceed** by responding to the situation rather than reacting.

This is a tool you can use in any situation to make space and allow urges to be present without acting on them. Some people find it helpful to put the acronym on a sticky note on their computer, fridge or car dash to remind them that this tool is available to them.

\<button\> Take action

Resource:  
[STOP micro meditation](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1fQ0prAYlXmDFgxDfJNfCDsJ2RvVbOzxt/view?usp=share_link)

#### Question page: {#question-page:-25}

Will you try this technique in moments when emotional urges are leading you toward reactions you may regret? 

* Yes  
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose 1\]

How could you remind yourself to use this tool? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]  
---

### S7M4  {#s7m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-36}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-36}

Radical Acceptance

#### Body: {#body:-35}

Radical acceptance is a practice that allows us to make space for our experience without trying to deny, resist or change it. It is particularly useful for major life changes and events that are simply beyond our control. Practicing radical acceptance during the uncomfortable or unpleasant situations in your daily life helps you integrate this tool into your toolbox so it’s ready to use for the big stuff.

#### (headline) Practice this tool when you encounter an inconvenience or annoyance: {#(headline)-practice-this-tool-when-you-encounter-an-inconvenience-or-annoyance:}

1. Acknowledge the situation as it is without judgment:  
   Example: *I am feeling stressed because there is a huge pile-up on the highway, and I am going to be late to my son’s school play.*

2. Ask the question: Is this a situation I can change or control? If not, am I willing to accept things as they are?  
   Example: *No, the traffic isn’t moving, and I am blocked in for now. I don’t want to accept it, but I don’t have a choice.*

3. Observe the situation without judgment – describe it as if you were an objective observer. Allow it to be what it is without trying to change it.  
   Example: *I am stuck in traffic and may miss part of my son’s performance. I feel disappointed and sad. I fear that my son will also be disappointed, but this is just one of those things that happens to all of us.* 

4. Remind yourself that you can’t change the situation. Take a few deep breaths with kindness as you allow yourself to simply be in the experience.   
   Example: *If I could change this situation I would, but I can’t.* 

\<button\> Take action

#### Question page: {#question-page:-26}

Will you try this technique in a moment of frustration? 

* Yes  
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose 1\]

How could you remind yourself to use this tool? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S7M5  {#s7m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-37}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-37}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### Body: {#body:-36}

#### 

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-6}

1. Emotions can be connected to past experiences or fears we have about the future.  
2. Certain emotions can be difficult because we have beliefs or judgments that they are “wrong” or “bad.”   
3. An important aspect of emotional regulation is recognizing your physical symptoms when your fear system is automatically reacting.  
4. You can use the STOP technique to shift to thoughtfully responding in difficult situations.   
5. Radical acceptance is a practice that allows us to make space for our experience without trying to deny, resist or change it.

#### 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-6}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it**. You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-27}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice noticing your physical fear mode symptoms  
* Practice identifying and unpacking complex emotions  
* Practice the STOP technique when you are in fear mode  
* Practice radical acceptance  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, Multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

## Session 8 {#session-8}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-7}

Behavior

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-7}

Session 8

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-7}

Learn how changing your behavior can affect your thoughts and emotions.

### S8M1 {#s8m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-38}

Track

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### \# Required {##-required}

3

Max per day:  
3

Tracking content:  
Log at least 3 times this week

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-38}

Activity Tracking

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-37}

Behavior change is the most critical element of CBT. By viewing depression as something we do rather than something we have, we emphasize that depression is also something we can do something about. This does not mean that the depression is your fault, but it does mean that you can take action. 

To learn how our behavior impacts our mood, we will use a CBT tool called activity tracking. Similar to the mood tracking you practiced earlier, activity tracking tracks your activities and your mood throughout the day.

Activity tracking is especially effective if you record your mood multiple times a day doing different activities. Take notes of what you were doing when you felt that way. It may be helpful to include details like the time of day or people you were with. 

In the next {{lesson}}, you will review your log and observe what you were doing when you felt your best and worst. Then you can practice intentionally integrating activities into your schedule to help improve your mood. 

\<button\> Track mood

Question Page:

How do you feel today? (select all that apply)

* Happy  
* Sad  
* Angry  
* Nervous/anxious  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what are you feeling? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How intense is your feeling or feelings? (optional)  
\[number picker 1-10\]

What were you doing when you felt this way? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Notes and observations: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

#### 

#### ---

#### 

### S8M2 {#s8m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-39}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-39}

Review Activity Log

#### Body: {#body:-38}

Review your mood and activity logs to see if you notice any patterns. Do you report feeling happier when you are around certain people or worse when you are doing certain activities? Are you in a better mood at certain times of day? Record anything that may help you to understand what impacts your mood.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-28}

After looking at your logs, do you notice any patterns? Do you tend to feel better in the morning or the evening? Did you feel noticeably better or worse when you were with certain people?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What were you doing when you rated your mood high? Why do these activities likely improve your mood? Are these activities or things that you could integrate more into your life?   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What were you doing when you rated your mood low? Why do these activities likely worsen your mood? Are these activities or things that you could change to improve your experience? Is there a way that you could accept or be kinder to yourself in these situations that may make the experience better?   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S8M3 {#s8m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-40}

Exercise

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-40}

Put an Action on It

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-39}

So far, we have practiced observing how our thoughts impact our mood and behaviors and relating to our emotions in more flexible ways. Research has found that, while these are essential tools for managing depression, **changing behavior is the critical tool that makes CBT so effective.** 

In other words, what we do, or in some cases what we choose not to do, perpetuates and maintains our depression. Knowing this allows us to intentionally notice the things we are doing (or not doing) that maintain our unique experience of depression and choose to do things differently. 

This may sound simple, but changing behaviors can be particularly difficult when you are depressed. To support making these changes, try making a plan to take actions that will help you to feel better and move forward with your life.

#### (headline) Identify and schedule positive activities in your daily life   {#(headline)-identify-and-schedule-positive-activities-in-your-daily-life }

Look at this list of positive activities and record any others that you think of. You may have already identified some positive activities in your activity tracking exercise. You may want to review your values as you choose your activities to ensure you are choosing activities that matter to you.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-29}

Select the activities that you would find pleasant or engaging: (select all that apply)

* Play with or walk a pet  
* Work in the garden  
* Spend time with friends or family  
* Watch a movie or play a video game  
* Take a walk or a hike  
* Sing or dance  
* Relax by taking a bath or meditating  
* Paint, draw or another art medium  
* Write in your journal  
* Practice gratitude  
* Engage in a hobby   
* Write a letter or email to an old friend   
* Bake or cook a new recipe  
* Listen to music or a podcast

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

What other activities (not listed) would you find pleasant and engaging? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Which 1 or 2 of these activities would you be willing to integrate into your regular life routine? Give this activity a time slot in your schedule so you can commit to doing it intentionally.   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S8M4 {#s8m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-41}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-41}

Goal-Setting

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-40}

Now that you have an activity or 2 you’d like to do more, let’s set a goal and make an action plan to achieve just one. 

(headline) Choose a goal  
Make your goal specific. For example, run 3 times per week, take a walk with a friend once per week, cook twice a week.

(headline) Break it into steps  
Chunk your goal into smaller, more achievable steps. Add these steps to your calendar and track your progress. For example, if your goal is to run 3 times per week, you might start by washing your exercise clothes, finding a running partner or taking a practice run.

(headline) Practice self-compassion  
As you work toward your goal, be kind to yourself if you miss taking a planned action or don’t complete as much as you intended to. It’s normal and okay if you slip up. You may also find that you need to adjust your goal as you go, and that’s okay too. Set a new intention and try again. Any progress is good progress, even setting the intention to take the action.

\<button\> Complete exercise 

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-30}

Your specific goal:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Step 1 toward your goal:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Step 2 toward your goal:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Step 3 toward your goal:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What might get in the way of you taking these steps toward this goal? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Is there someone you could ask to help support your decision to achieve this goal? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S8M5 {#s8m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-42}

Exercise

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-42}

Emotional Urges

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-41}

Many emotions can lead to an urge. These emotional urges can “take over” our brains, causing us to behave in ways that “feel right” in the moment but that we regret later. For example, while driving in rush hour traffic, a person gets cut off. They react in anger by honking their horn and swearing at the other driver before noticing the shocked looks of their kids in the back seat. 

#### Here is a list of emotions and the urges that tend to be connected to them: {#here-is-a-list-of-emotions-and-the-urges-that-tend-to-be-connected-to-them:}

* Anger can lead to the urge to attack or defend.  
* Sadness can lead to the urge to isolate or withdraw.  
* Fear can lead to the urge to fight or flee.  
* Irritation can lead to the urge to lash out or be passive-aggressive.  
* Shame can lead to the urge to hide or avoid.

These urges are the default settings in your fear-mode brain. This is why they may feel right in the moment, but usually lead us to behave in ways we regret after the urge has passed. Recognizing these urges gives you the tool to intentionally override this setting. Then you can pause and consider other options before choosing how to respond to the situation. It can take some practice, but over time you will see the value of responding rather than reacting.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-31}

Think back to a situation that happened when you reacted in a way that you regretted. What was the situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What was the emotion and reaction in this situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How did it turn out?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Can you see how reacting feels right in the moment but looks different after the fact? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S8M6  {#s8m6}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-43}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-43}

Urge Surfing

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-42}

Behaviors are most problematic when we simply react to the urge rather than pausing to choose a response. This tool helps us learn to “surf” our urges, allowing them to take their natural course without making them worse by trying to resist, judge or change them. 

“You can’t stop the waves, but you can learn how to **surf.**” Dr.  Jon Kabat-Zinn, founder of mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)

1. **S**\- Make **space** for and acknowledge the situation objectively. *(Another car just hit my bumper. I am feeling angry this happened and afraid that something is wrong with my car.)*

2. **U**\- Acknowledge the **urge** and that it is linked to how you are feeling. *(I feel the urge to yell at this person. Hello anger, nice to see you. Fear, so sorry you’re back again…*). Remember that urges are like waves \- they rise, peak and eventually fall.

3. **R**\- **Remain** present with the feeling, allowing it to be what it is in each moment as it changes and shifts. *(The anger has shifted to annoyance and the fear for my car has shifted to gratefulness that I am unharmed.)*

4. **F**\- Just as waves **fall**, acknowledge that your urge will eventually subside. *(As I recognize that insurance will pay for the damage and that I am alright, I can move on with my day.)* 

This is a tool you can use in any situation to make space and allow urges to be present without acting on them. Some people find it helpful to put the acronym on a sticky note on their computer, fridge or car dash to remind them that this tool is available to them.

\<button\> Take action

#### Question page: {#question-page:-32}

Will you try this technique in moments when emotional urges are leading you toward reactions you may regret? 

* Yes  
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose 1\]

How could you remind yourself to use this tool? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S8M7  {#s8m7}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-44}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-44}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-43}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-7}

1. Behavior change is the most critical element of CBT.   
2. By conceptualizing depression as something we do rather than something we have, we emphasize that depression is something we can do something about.   
3. Keeping track of your mood throughout the day can help you determine what activities raise and lower your mood.  
4. Add activities you enjoy to your schedule to ensure you will do them.  
5. Breaking goals into smaller, more manageable pieces helps you stay on track.  
6. Urges are the default settings in your fear-mode brain that may feel right in the moment, but usually lead us to behave in ways we regret after the urge has passed.   
7. Pausing when you get an urge can help you to respond thoughtfully rather than react automatically.

#### 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-7}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned and select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it.** You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action 

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-33}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice activity tracking  
* Identify activities that make you feel positive  
* Schedule positive activities in your day  
* Set goals and chunk these goals into smaller steps  
* Pause before giving in to urges  
* Practice urge surfing  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, Multi-select\]

#### ---

---

## Session 9 {#session-9}

#### Session title:  {#session-title:-8}

Helpful Actions

#### Session attribute:  {#session-attribute:-8}

Session 9

#### Session description:  {#session-description:-8}

Learn more actions you can take to manage your depression.

### S9M1 {#s9m1}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-45}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-45}

Opposite Action Exercise

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-44}

Sometimes our depression tells us to do exactly the opposite of what will make us feel better. We feel lonely and isolated, yet our depression tells us to avoid friends and family. Or, we feel irritable and angry, so we lash out at the people we love. These reactions result in a vicious cycle that just leads us to feel worse\! 

Example situation: *I didn’t go to my friend's birthday party because I felt too tired and down.*

Now let’s consider what you might have done if you had used the opposite action tool.

Example response: *I could have gone to the party, even though I was tired, and stayed for an hour. I would probably feel awkward at first, but I would be glad to be there for my friend and have seen a few people that I haven’t seen for a while. I wouldn’t have felt bad for missing the party and disappointing my friend.*

Now try this exercise on a situation from your own life.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-34}

Think of a situation when you did or did not do something that you now regret. What was the situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What was the emotion and urge you acted on?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What would have been the opposite action(s) you could have taken in the situation?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

What would have been different if you had chosen the opposite action?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Now that you have imagined using this tool, you are one step closer to being able to try it in your life.  Will you try this tool out when a situation comes up?

* Yes  
* No

\[Multiple choice, choose 1\]

---

###  S9M2  {#s9m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-46}

Exercise

**Audio:**  
No

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-46}

What Are You Avoiding?

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-45}

Sometimes the things that we do impact our mood and wellbeing, but research shows that sometimes the things we **don’t** do also impact our mood and wellbeing. Identify the activities, experiences and areas of your life that you are neglecting or avoiding.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-35}

Take a look at the list of typical avoidant behaviors that maintain depression and select all that apply to you. 

* Isolating yourself from friends and family   
* Missing work or school   
* Avoiding working on assignments or projects   
* Withdrawing from your partner   
* Saying no to new opportunities or activities   
* Avoiding activities and interests you once enjoyed  
* Deciding not to attend events with family or friends   
* Avoiding situations that make you uncomfortable  
* Using substances to cope with unpleasant emotions   
* Putting off important tasks   
* Not engaging in self-care activities  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

What other experiences and activities are you avoiding: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How could you implement an opposite action for one of these avoidant behaviors?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S9M3  {#s9m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-47}

Action

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-47}

Social Withdrawal

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-46}

Avoiding contact with the people we care about most is nearly always a part of the experience of depression. It is a great example of how depression often tells us to do the opposite of what would make us feel better. 

When you notice that you are isolating yourself from opportunities to engage with others, consider these simple formulas:

#### (headline) **When in Doubt, Reach Out** \-  {#(headline)-when-in-doubt,-reach-out--}

Reach out to friends, family, coworkers, caregivers, professional support, etc.

#### (headline) **When in Doubt, Go Out** \-  {#(headline)-when-in-doubt,-go-out--}

Choose to get out of the house and into the world. This could mean taking a walk, getting yourself a treat or going to work rather than staying in bed.

#### (headline) **When in Doubt, Help Out** \-  {#(headline)-when-in-doubt,-help-out--}

The research is clear on this one: Volunteering or caring for others in need has benefits for the giver as well as the recipient. People usually predict that engaging in volunteer activities to help others will be hard, uncomfortable or tiring, but the research finds that volunteering is correlated with increased positive feelings of competence and wellbeing. 

Don’t forget to take your values into account. If one of your values is service and community, it may be especially beneficial for you to volunteer. If your top value is health and wellness, taking a run around your neighborhood might make you feel your best.

\<button\> Take action

Question page:

What is one way you could reach out, go out or help out?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

When will you do it?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S9M4  {#s9m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-48}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-48}

Self-Care

#### 

#### Body:  {#body:-47}

Often our emotions are dysregulated because we are tired, hungry, stressed, etc. Self-care is doing the things that will allow you to be at your best in challenging times. Some people assume that self-care means allowing yourself to get pedicures and massages. While that may feel good, it may not be the self-care you need if you are stressed because you are late on an assignment at work or haven’t done your laundry in a month. Sometimes, self-care looks like going to bed early for a few nights rather than watching a movie, because lack of sleep has a huge impact on your mood. 

Yet, self-care isn’t all about telling yourself what you should be doing, but about gently encouraging yourself to do the things that are likely to help you feel better and make your life feel more manageable. For instance: cleaning off your desk, asking your boss for a few days off so you can finish moving into your new apartment or saying no to your friend’s request to take care of her dog isn’t what we typically see as self-care. Nonetheless, these are the kinds of things that can help you to feel more balanced and grounded in your life.

\<button\> Take action

#### Question page: {#question-page:-36}

What forms of self-care will you engage in this week?

* Focusing on getting a full night’s sleep   
* Focusing on eating well  
* Practicing mindfulness, meditation or yoga regularly  
* Maintaining a regular routine of physical activity  
* Maintaining your boundaries and saying “no”  
* Taking time for rest and relaxation  
* Taking time for household errands and organization      
* Other 

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what will you do? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S9M5 {#s9m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-49}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-49}

Gratitude Journal

#### Body: {#body:-48}

The final tool we will learn to help you move through your depression is gratitude journaling. Research has shown that using a gratitude journal to intentionally notice and record what you are grateful for can help reduce stress, boost positive emotions and improve relationships. It can be as simple as committing to writing down 3 things you are grateful for each day. Let’s try it now\! 

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-37}

I am grateful for:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

I am grateful for:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

I am grateful for:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Do you think you could integrate this practice into your daily routine? What might get in the way? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Try doing it at the same time of day and keeping your journal in the same place to remind you to do your gratitude journal entries. It may also help to pair this new habit with something you do every day, like brushing your teeth or stretching before bed. How will you integrate this into your daily routine? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---

### S9M6 {#s9m6}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-50}

Action

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-50}

Takeaways and Action Plan

#### 

#### Body: {#body:-49}

#### (headline) Takeaways: {#(headline)-takeaways:-8}

1. Sometimes our depression tells us to do exactly the opposite of what will make us feel better. Try doing the opposite of what it tells you to do.  
2. Neglecting and avoiding activities that are important to you impacts your mood and wellbeing.    
3. Social isolation can significantly contribute to your depression. When in doubt, get out of the house or reach out to friends and loved ones.   
4. Helping out family, friends or your community can increase your wellbeing.  
5. Self-care is doing the things that will allow you to be at your best in challenging times.  
6. Practicing gratitude can help reduce stress, boost positive emotions and improve relationships.

#### 

#### (headline) Action plan: {#(headline)-action-plan:-8}

Take a look at the list of things that you learned. Select the items you would be willing to do to **put an action on it**. You may be able to think of some of your own as well. 

\<button\> Take action

#### 

#### Question page: {#question-page:-38}

Select all of the items that you might be willing to integrate into your life moving forward:

* Practice the opposite action tool  
* Start or restart activities I have been avoiding   
* Reach out to friends and family   
* Go out instead of staying in  
* Try volunteering or helping my community  
* Practice gratitude journaling  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, Multi-select\]

Other actions I can take to begin to manage my depression: (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

---
