### S10M2  {#s10m2}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-52}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-52}

Recognizing Setbacks

#### Body: {#body:-51}

Now that you have a better understanding of depression and are integrating the concepts and tools you learned into your life, you may want to charge right back into your life. It is important to know that if setbacks happen, and they often do, you have the skills and knowledge to manage your depression differently. Setbacks happen, but with some planning, you can recognize them early and get back on track. 

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-40}

These are common early warning signs that you may be having a setback. Take a minute to select any that you experienced in the past:

* Getting too much or not enough sleep  
* Loss of appetite or overeating  
* Increased feelings of sadness or hopelessness  
* Being more irritable or angry than usual  
* Withdrawing from friends and family  
* Thoughts of self-harm or suicide  
* Excessive rumination and worry  
* Conflict at home or work  
* Decreased engagement with self-care  
* Increased use of alcohol or other substances  
* Change of seasons  
* Hormonal changes  
* Stressful life events  
* Loss or grief  
* Other health concerns

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

Let’s add a few more things to this list that may be unique to your particular presentation of depression. You can look back in your journal or just think back to when you were feeling depressed. Are there any other warning signs that you can recognize? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]  
---

### S10M3 {#s10m3}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-53}

Exercise

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### 

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-53}

Your Takeaways

Each {{session}} included key takeaways to review the important skills, insights and concepts. Review the takeaways and note any that you would like to carry forward into your life.

1. Depression is one of the most well-researched and common mental disorders, experienced by people from every walk of life. CBT is considered one of the most effective treatments.  
2. Depression is NOT your fault. Depression can be caused by a multitude of factors, like genetics, hormonal changes, past trauma and current stressors and transitions.   
3. Depression often leads us to behave in ways that make us more depressed, leading to a vicious cycle.  
4. Mindfulness and self-compassion are the foundations of current CBT. The key to mindfulness is staying grounded in the present moment.   
5. Difficult feelings are usually linked to NATs about ourselves, others and the world around us. Noticing NATs can help us recognize that thoughts are not facts.   
6. Depression creates a negative confirmation bias by favoring evidence that confirms negative thoughts and dismisses evidence that disproves these beliefs. You can break this cycle by collecting evidence to disprove your NATs and support new, more balanced thoughts.  
7. Difficult emotions are an unavoidable part of the human experience. Attempts to avoid the discomfort of difficult emotions can prolong and intensify these difficult emotions.   
8. Learning to be with and tolerate the discomfort of difficult emotions is a critical part of learning how to relate to our emotions differently.   
9. Sometimes our depression tells us to do exactly the opposite of what will make us feel better. Try doing the opposite of what it tells you to do. Neglecting and avoiding activities that are important to us impacts our mood and wellbeing.  
10. Self-care is doing the things that will allow you to be at your best in challenging times.

Think about tangible ways to integrate these concepts into your life moving forward. If you were to describe what you learned to a friend, what are the top 3 takeaway concepts you would share with them? Explain in your own words.

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page:  {#question-page:-41}

Takeaway concept \#1:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Takeaway concept \#2:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Takeaway concept \#3:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Why do you find these ideas particularly helpful? Are there ways that you can remind yourself to use these concepts when you are having a challenging day or feeling more down than usual? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Let’s make a plan to help you do the things you need to do to manage the setback. Imagine future you and how you might feel when you are having a setback. Knowing what you know now, what would you say to yourself to help you move forward and get back on track?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

### S10M4  {#s10m4}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-54}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
No

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-54}

Tools and Resources

#### Body: {#body:-52}

#### (headline) Create a coping card  {#(headline)-create-a-coping-card}

Sometimes it is really hard to summon our tools and skills in the moment. That is why it can be very helpful to select 3 – 5 tools that are particularly helpful to you. Make a note of them and keep them in a place where they are readily available to you. When you are in a challenging moment, you have this to remind you of things that have helped you in the past.

Don’t forget the tools and resources we covered are available to you at any time.  

\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-42}

Select the tools listed below that you think will be most helpful to you when you are having a setback:  

* Mood tracking  
* Activity tracking  
* 5 senses grounding technique  
* Emotional acceptance meditation  
* 4-7-8 breathing exercise  
* Self-Compassion meditation   
* Shift from scare system to care system  
* Identifying and catching NATs and thought errors  
* Cognitive defusion   
* Expanding your emotional vocabulary   
* Thought record  
* Shift from worry to problem-solving  
* Radical acceptance  
* STOP technique  
* Urge surfing  
* Opposite action tool  
* Gratitude journaling  
* When in doubt, reach out, get out or help out  
* Other

\[Multiple choice, multi-select\]

If other, what tool will be helpful when experiencing a setback? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Coping card tool \#1:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Coping card tool \#2:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Coping card tool \#3:  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

### S10M5 {#s10m5}

#### Module type: {#module-type:-55}

Exercise

#### 

**Audio:**  
Yes

#### Module title:  {#module-title:-55}

Reaching Out

#### Body: {#body:-53}

One of the most common warning signs that you are having a setback is when you notice that you are avoiding contact with friends and family. That is why it is important to let your support system know that you would appreciate their help if they notice you are struggling. Who are the people you are willing to reach out to in order to let them know what a setback might look like and what they can do to help?

#### (headline) When to seek professional help {#(headline)-when-to-seek-professional-help}

Sometimes depression symptoms worsen and the best plan is to reach out for professional help. For example, if your symptoms worsen and you find that you are unable to meet your daily obligations, or if you are having thoughts of self-harm or suicide. 

If this is the case, who would you reach out to? Consider:

* Your healthcare provider, to discuss how you are feeling and what might help  
* Mental health resources in your community  
* Your support team, to help you get the support you need

If you are having thoughts of self-harm or suicide, the best thing to do is to tell a family member or someone you trust that you are having these thoughts and ask them to contact emergency services or take you to the closest emergency hospital. In the U S, call 911, call or text 988 to reach the 24-hour National Suicide Helpline or go to an emergency room.  
\<button\> Complete exercise

#### Question page: {#question-page:-43}

What is the name of one person you will reach out to for support?   
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How can they be helpful? When will you talk to them about this?  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

Who else would you like to reach out to for support? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]

How can they be helpful? When will you talk to them about this? (optional)  
\[Open answer with validation 26\]
